full_text:
REVISION [[Fernando Perez]{.underline}](mailto:frpvillatoro@gmail.com)

Reviewer #3 (Comments to the Author ):

The authors have addressed most of my comments to satisfaction, I only
have one remark.

The authors present new data supporting notion that MHCII is expressed
by tumor cells and not detected due to spillover from imaging. While
this is appreciated and supported by their functional experiments, I am
wondering why in Supp 3g and in the point-by-point response, the values
for MHCII on cancer cells don't line up between the different
correlation analyses (i.e. the correlations with CD11b, MHCII and CD3).
In the MHCII in cancer vs. neighbor plot there are lots of cancer MHCII
values between 11 and 15 while these are missing from the cancer MHCII
vs max(CD3d or CD11b). Where different input cells used here? The
authors should show this data for all tumor cells.

We appreciate the reviewer\'s comment, and we agree that we did not add
important descriptions of our analysis before and this can lead to
confusion. We added a clarification in the figure legend according to
our next comments.

Our previous analysis was performed using a random downsampling of 500
cancer cells. We adopted this approach because when all cancer cells
were included (N ≈ 2 million), the high dispersion in the data precluded
reliable visualization of correlated trends, as shown in the two figures

ach because when all cancer cells
were included (N ≈ 2 million), the high dispersion in the data precluded
reliable visualization of correlated trends, as shown in the two figures
below that include all the cancer cells. Importantly, according to the
central limit theorem for correlated data, random sampling preserves the
statistical properties of the original distribution. This principle has
also been discussed and demonstrated in the context of spatial tumor
profiling by Jia-Ren Lin et al. (2023; PMID: 36669472), specifically in
Figures 2F and 2H. To assess the robustness of this downsampling
strategy, we performed the following in-silico experiment. Downsampling
was conducted 10,000 times, with 500 cancer cells sampled per replicate.
For each replicate, the Pearson correlation coefficient (R) was
calculated between the expression of the indicated markers and MHCII in
the sampled cancer cells. The mean R across the 10,000 replicates is
reported together with the corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CI)
in the next table. In addition, the mean p-value and its confidence
intervals for testing the deviation of R from zero. As observed, the
mean correlations and confidence intervals across replicates fall within
the range of correlations previously reported in Supplementary Figure
S5g and are consistent with results obtained using the full dataset. We

tervals across replicates fall within
the range of correlations previously reported in Supplementary Figure
S5g and are consistent with results obtained using the full dataset. We
therefore consider that displaying a downsampling is a more faithful
representation of the data for visualization purposes. Now in
Supplementary Figure S5g we added further details that the image
corresponds to a downsampling and added in addition the R calculated
using all cancer cells.

A similar in silico experiment measuring the Pearson correlation
coefficient (R) between the expression of the indicated markers in
cancer cells and the mean expression in neighboring immune cells within
a 20-µm radius demonstrating high concordance between each replicate.
The downsampling approach also shows high as when using all cancer cells
(see figure panels below).

*#Done [[Alexandra
Shabanova]{.underline}](mailto:alexandras.emailbox@gmail.com) please add
this new figure legend to Supp Figure 5:*

**g** Scatter plot showing the intensity of immune markers (CD3d and
CD11b) versus MHCII intensity in cancer cells. The figure displays the
Pearson correlation coefficient (R), the correlation test p-value, and
the linear regression line (blue) based on a downsample of 500 cells.
Downsampling was preferred for visualization purposes, when all cancer
cells are included (N ≈ 2 million), the correlation with MHCII is R =
0.26 for CD11b and R = 0.25 for CD3d, respectively.

OPENER ART [[Anniina Färkkilä]{.underline}](mailto:afarkkila@gmail.com)

-One of our graphic designers will be creating "opener art" for your
article, an artistic illustration that will appear on the article\'s

.underline}](mailto:afarkkila@gmail.com)

-One of our graphic designers will be creating "opener art" for your
article, an artistic illustration that will appear on the article\'s
front page and on promotional materials. If you have suggestions about
the color, concept, or particular images for the artwork, please send
them within the next 48 hours for editorial consideration. Ideas that
are consistent with journal style will be passed on to the designers for
potential use at the editors\' discretion.

We have been using in our figures these colors: #508791, #C5DEF7,
#FED18C, #A7AEF2, #D33F49, #D9DEE4, #6599CD. Hopefully, they can be
useful for you, Anniina Färkkilä

#DONE FUNDING
[[joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com]{.underline}](mailto:joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com)

During resubmission, it is critical that you enter into the submission
system all grant-based funding information for this work, as
appropriate. You may also request that the AACR assist you in fulfilling
funding agency manuscript deposition requirements by depositing the
accepted version of your manuscript on your behalf in PMC or Europe PMC.
To do both, go to the \"Detailed Information\" page of the submission
forms, scroll down to the \"Funding and Manuscript Deposition\" section
and follow the instructions.

#DONE For iCAN funding: The iCAN project is funded by the Research

submission
forms, scroll down to the \"Funding and Manuscript Deposition\" section
and follow the instructions.

#DONE For iCAN funding: The iCAN project is funded by the Research
Council of Finland (grant numbers 346555 and 369117), University of
Helsinki, Helsinki University Hospital and industry partner Boehringer
Ingelheim.

AUTHOR FORMS
[[joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com]{.underline}](mailto:joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com)

The AACR requires all authors to complete, as a condition of formal
acceptance, two individual electronic forms: a disclosure form and
either a copyright form or suitable open access license. With this
decision letter, our office has emailed the disclosure form to each
author of this manuscript, asking him or her to complete it as soon as
possible. During the revision process, you will select either the
electronic copyright form or an appropriate open access license to
trigger. Our office must receive all forms to proceed with formal
acceptance and publication.

Email for iCAN: [ican@helsinki.fi]{.mark}

#AWAITING AACR CONFIRMATION EMAILS
[[joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com]{.underline}](mailto:joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com)

To facilitate the process of form collection, please ensure that all
author email addresses in our online submission system are accurate.
Notify our office of any changes to email addresses immediately.

AUTHORSHIP CHANGE FORM REQUIRED

ure that all
author email addresses in our online submission system are accurate.
Notify our office of any changes to email addresses immediately.

AUTHORSHIP CHANGE FORM REQUIRED
[[joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com]{.underline}](mailto:joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com)

\- The following change has been made to the author list: authors added.

\- A representative of the iCAN group will need to sign this form.

\- When authorship changes are made during revision, you must submit an
authorship change form signed by every author, including any author(s)
who have been added or removed. Note that authorship changes constitute
the addition or removal of contributors; the modification of the author
listing order; and the modification of co-authorship.

\- A blank authorship change form is available here:
[[https://aacrjournals.org/DocumentLibrary/Authorship_Change_Form.pdf]{.underline}](https://aacrjournals.org/DocumentLibrary/Authorship_Change_Form.pdf)

\- As the corresponding author, you are ultimately responsible for
ensuring the accuracy of the author list. Please email the form, signed
by all authors, to cancerdiscovery@aacr.org.

ARTICLE FILE

\- To ensure the editors have the most up-to-date version of the
manuscript, please upload a \"clean\" version that does not contain any
editorial marks.

\- Figures should be supplied as separate files and not contained within
the main text Word document.

\- Please upload the main text figures as separate graphic files, such
as AI, EPS, PNG, PPT, TIFF, etc. We cannot accept PDFs or JPGs for the
main text figures.#DONE

SUPPLEMENTARY DATA [[Alexandra
Shabanova]{.underline}](mailto:alexandras.emailbox@gmail.com)#DONE

\- If the extended figures and tables are to be published, please upload
them as "Supplementary Data" files. Please also change their labels to
"Figure S\[#\]" and "Table S\[#\]" and add them to the supplementary
data files.

--- For journal purposes, "supplementary" data and "extended" data are
considered the same thing.

\- Every Supplementary Data file must have a title that matches the main
text callout (e.g., Supplementary Figure S1).

\- Every Supplementary Data file must contain a brief caption in its
\"Description\" field on the submission form. These captions should
describe each file\'s contents without reiterating the title (i.e., it
is acceptable to provide \"Supplementary Figure S1,\" for example, as a
title but NOT as a caption; instead, the caption should read,

tents without reiterating the title (i.e., it
is acceptable to provide \"Supplementary Figure S1,\" for example, as a
title but NOT as a caption; instead, the caption should read,
\"Supplementary Figure S1 shows x, y, and z\...\").

\- Table S14 is not cited in the main text.

\- Figures S4M, S5L are cited out of order in the main text.

\- Extended Figure 3A-B are cited out of order in the main text.

\- Extended Table 1 is missing.

\- Every supplementary figure, table, and data file must be cited and
numbered, in order, in the main text.

#AWAITING AACR CONFIRMATION AUTHOR NAME DISCREPANCY
[[joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com]{.underline}](mailto:joonas.jj.jukonen@gmail.com)

\- The author list in the manuscript file \[MS\] must match the one
listed in the submission system \[SS\]. The author names listed in
SmartSubmit are the ones that will appear in the article metadata
published online first and deposited in PubMed. Please address the
discrepancy by correcting either the form or the manuscript.

\- As the corresponding author, you are ultimately responsible for
ensuring the accuracy of the author list as it appears in SmartSubmit
and in the manuscript.

\- If the error(s) is in the manuscript file, please correct it and
upload a revised version. If the error(s) is in the submission system,
please contact our office at cancerdiscovery@aacr.org. Only the

n the manuscript file, please correct it and
upload a revised version. If the error(s) is in the submission system,
please contact our office at cancerdiscovery@aacr.org. Only the
individual author or a journal staff member can change an author's
account. You will be unable to do so in the submission form.
